model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The budget-aware cosine schedule already improved loss, so the next clean lever is modestly increasing MLP capacity. Raising the base width from 192 to 256 hidden units should improve representation quality within the same training loop while keeping the architecture simple and still small enough for the fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last width increase helped, which suggests the MLP is still capacity-limited. Adding one more hidden block is the next simple architecture step and should improve feature composition while preserving the existing optimizer and schedule that are already working well.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"This run is fixed by training steps rather than epochs, so each step should cover more examples when possible. Doubling the batch size is a simple way to expose the MLP to substantially more data within the same 256-step budget, and the current model remains small enough that this may still fit comfortably in the checker time envelope.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The fixed-step setting strongly favored a larger batch, so the next highest-value follow-up is to push that lever once more. Increasing to 256 should let each of the 256 optimizer steps cover twice as many examples again, which may improve validation loss more than smaller optimizer tweaks if runtime remains acceptable.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The larger fixed-step batches produced the strongest gains so far, which usually leaves room for a higher learning rate. With batch size now at 256 plus cosine decay and gradient clipping, a moderate LR increase is a high-value next test to improve optimization speed without changing the overall training design.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The higher learning rate regressed validation loss, so the next step should back out that failed scalar change and return to the last known good optimization scale. The batch-size increase was the real source of the recent gain, and restoring the previous LR is the cleanest way to preserve it before testing other directions.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current setup already benefits from larger batches and a matched cosine schedule, so the next clean lever is optimizer family. AdamW often works better than Adam once model size and batch size increase because decoupled weight decay tends to preserve optimization speed while improving generalization.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"AdamW did not help, so this candidate restores the better optimizer and then tests a lighter regularization level. With full-data training and only 256 steps, the current model is more likely under-optimized than overfit, so reducing weight decay is a plausible way to preserve the gains from the larger batch while improving fit.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The pattern remains clear: larger batches help substantially in this fixed-step regime. Since 384 improved again and runtime is still manageable, the next best experiment is to push once more to 512 and see whether more examples per optimizer step continues to dominate other tuning levers.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The dominant empirical trend is still batch scaling under the fixed 256-step evaluator. Since 512 improved again and runtime remains acceptable, moving to 768 is a measured next step that tests whether more examples per update continue to outweigh the reduced stochasticity without taking as aggressive a jump as 1024.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch scaling is still the strongest and most consistent signal in this run. Since 768 improved again despite the longer wall time, the next direct test is 1024 to check whether this fixed-step workload continues to reward maximizing examples per update before exploring more delicate optimizer or architecture changes.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Batch size has been scaled up by 16x from the starting point, and the optimization regime may now be under-driving updates at the original learning rate. A moderate increase to 1.5e-3 is a safer retest than the earlier 2e-3 jump and is more likely to match the much larger batch without overshooting.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The higher learning rate did not help, so this candidate restores the better scalar setting and spends the next step on capacity instead. With the optimizer now seeing very large batches and multiple effective passes over the data, a moderate width increase is a plausible way to extract more from the fixed 256 updates without changing the overall training recipe.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 4","old":"DEPTH = 3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The latest gain came from another moderate capacity increase, so the next clean follow-up is one more hidden block. With large batches and a stable optimizer setup already in place, extra depth is a plausible way to improve representation quality within the same fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The model is still improving from capacity increases even after the batch-size scaling, and the last depth increase remained positive. With only a few steps left, the most direct next experiment is a moderate additional width increase to see whether the current optimizer and large-batch regime can capitalize on a larger representation before more subtle tuning.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 32","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Capacity increases are still improving validation loss, and with only two steps left the most direct high-upside move is one more width jump. Raising the base width to 32 materially increases the early hidden layers while staying within the same overall training recipe, giving the large-batch optimizer a stronger model to fit.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 5","old":"DEPTH = 4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last width increase overshot slightly, so this candidate restores the better width setting and spends the final step on extra depth instead. Earlier depth increases were consistently helpful, and at BASE_CHANNELS 24 a fifth block is a plausible way to improve representation quality without repeating the width regression.","secondary_modes":[]}
